content: Imagine you are building a high-throughput in-memory queue for a distributed system where every thread must append and dequeue items without any blocking on locks. You have a single pointer, let's call it tail_ptr, pointing to the last element of your doubly-linked list stored in a Vec or raw slice. Every time Thread A tries to add an item, it reads tail_ptr, allocates memory for a new node, links it behind the current tail, and then attempts to write tail_ptr back with its address. Meanwhile, Thread B is simultaneously reading that same old value of tail_ptr from main memory, allocating its own node, linking it, and trying to update tail_ptr. When both threads finish their writes in an unpredictable order, the system might end up with a cycle where A's new node points to B's node, which then points back to A's node, creating a deadlock or corrupt data structure. This is the classic ABA problem: the value read by Thread A looks identical to the value read by Thread B, but the state of memory between those reads has completely changed, causing logic errors that standard atomic operations like compare-and-swap cannot prevent because they only guarantee visibility and ordering, not a consistent snapshot of the object's identity. To solve this without locks, you must introduce a tagged pointer mechanism where each address includes an additional counter field stored alongside the actual memory location in your Vec or raw slice. When Thread A reads tail_ptr, it captures both the data pointer and the current counter value. After modifying its node, it performs a compare-and-swap operation on the full 64-bit integer representing the tagged pointer; if the counter hasn't changed since the read, the swap succeeds and the new node is linked correctly. If the counter has advanced because another thread modified memory in between, Thread A must retry the entire sequence until the comparison matches exactly. This transforms your simple pointer variable into a compound value that acts as a unique identifier for every version of your list tail. The compiler treats this tagged integer as a single atomic unit when you use unsafe code to access its components; it ensures that operations on the combined 64-bit field are indivisible, preventing partial reads where one thread sees an old data pointer while another sees a new counter value. This is why we cannot simply store separate fields for the address and the counter in our Vec or raw slice without using atomic types like AtomicUsize or AtomicU64 to wrap them; if they were independent variables, the compiler would allow reordering where the write to the data pointer happens after the read of the data pointer but before the read of the counter, breaking the invariant that a thread sees either an old address with its old counter or a new address with its new counter. Memory ordering becomes critical here because we need to ensure that once Thread A reads the tagged value, all subsequent writes from other threads become visible to it immediately, and any write by Thread A is immediately visible to others before it completes; this requires using memory orderings like Acquire for loads and Release for stores in our unsafe blocks. We cannot rely on the default ordering because modern compilers and hardware might reorder instructions to optimize performance, which would allow a thread to observe an old data pointer paired with a new counter value from another thread's perspective, effectively breaking the tag-based consistency guarantee we built into our solution. In practice, when implementing this in Rust, you define your node structure as a simple array of u64 where the first half holds the memory address and the second half holds the sequence number, then wrap that entire 128-bit or 64-bit entity in an AtomicU64 to ensure atomicity across all operations. The proof of correctness relies on showing that if two threads perform a successful compare-and-swap, they must have read identical tagged values before their writes, which implies no other thread could have modified the memory between those reads without changing the counter, thus preserving the linear structure of your MPSC. This approach eliminates the need for any mutual exclusion lock entirely because every operation is self-contained and atomic at the hardware level, making it truly scalable for thousands of concurrent threads accessing a shared queue without contention on a central mutex resource.
